vulnerability.title: Enable Certmap in SSSD
vulnerability.description: SSSD should be configured to verify the certificate of the user or group. To set this up ensure that section like certmap/testing.test/rule_name is setup in /etc/sssd/sssd.conf. For example [certmap/testing.test/rule_name] matchrule =<SAN>.*EDIPI@mil maprule = (userCertificate;binary={cert!bin}) domains = testing.test
vulnerability.recommendation: Review and remediate: Enable Certmap in SSSD

triage_decision.reason: Enabling a certmap in SSSD is a standard authentication configuration change. It involves editing a single config file and restarting the sssd service, which is unlikely to cause lockout or system breakage if performed correctly.
triage_decision.estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; misconfiguration could prevent user authentication; incorrect syntax may stop sssd service

review_feedback: Adding a new certmap section without backing up the original file, validating the syntax, or checking SELinux contexts can cause sssd to fail to start. If sssd is used for authentication (e.g., SSH, sudo, login), a failure would lock out users and interrupt services. The plan also does not verify that the referenced domain exists, which could further break authentication.

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: The plan will add the required certmap section and restart SSSD, which should enable certificate mapping. However, it should backup the original config and validate the syntax before restarting. Also ensure SELinux context and file ownership.
concerns: No syntax validation of the added section
Potential SELinux context issues after file edit
Restart may cause brief authentication interruption
Does not verify that the domain 'testing.test' is defined
suggested_improvements: Create a backup copy of sssd.conf (e.g., cp /etc/sssd/sssd.conf /etc/sssd/sssd.conf.bak) before editing
Run 'sssd -i -d 5' or 'sssd -t' to test the configuration after changes
Ensure file ownership is root:root and SELinux context is correct (restorecon -v /etc/sssd/sssd.conf)
Consider using 'systemctl reload sssd' instead of restart to avoid downtime
Confirm that the domain 'testing.test' exists in the [domain/... ] section
